item.title: #3514748: Remove legacy browser support from js.module.css
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +7/−26 · css +6/−25 · php +1/−1
  • Diff: 5b74413
  • Discussion: #3514748 · 12 contributors · 62 comments
  • Followers: 16

Summary

The js.module.css stylesheet no longer uses .js class-based selectors to control JavaScript-dependent visibility. All visibility logic is now expressed purely through standard CSS @media (scripting: enabled) and @media (scripting: none) queries, which are well-supported across Drupal's supported browsers. The .js-hide rule now carries display: none !important to ensure it overrides other display rules regardless of selector specificity. The stylesheet is slightly smaller as a result. There is no user-facing effect on supported browsers.

Impact

Affects front-end developers.

  • Theme change: .js-hide now uses display: none !important; any custom CSS that overrides .js-hide without !important to reveal an element will no longer take effect.

Technical details

The original js.module.css had two layers of visibility rules, introduced in #3404218 to fix a layout-shift regression. The first layer used .js .js-hide and .js .js-show selectors that relied on JavaScript adding a .js class to the <html> element. The second layer used @media (scripting: enabled) blocks with a doubled .js-hide.js-hide selector specifically to out-rank the first layer.

This issue removes the legacy first layer entirely. Since the @media (scripting: ...) media feature is now broadly supported across all of Drupal's required browsers, the .js-class fallback is no longer needed.

The resulting rules are:

  • @media (scripting: none) { .js-show { display: none !important } }: prevents JavaScript-only content from showing in no-JS environments.
  • @media (scripting: enabled) { .js-hide { display: none !important } }: hides no-JS content when scripting is active.

The display: none !important is deliberate. An initial commit (f9be36f0) was merged and then immediately reverted after lendude discovered that buttons in the Views wizard were unexpectedly visible. Catch traced the root cause to a CSS specificity conflict: Claro's .button { display: inline-block } was winning over the bare .js-hide selector once the higher-specificity .js .js-hide prefix was gone. The thread debated three remedies: the existing Drupal core convention .js-hide.js-hide, the Drupal CMS convention .js-hide[class], and !important. After jwilson3 noted that the change simultaneously removed all display: block usages from .js-show, making .js-hide/.js-show exclusively about hiding, catch concluded that !important is semantically appropriate here and is justified as a real CSS feature rather than a hack.

The display: block rule for .js-show inside @media (scripting: enabled) is also removed. Elements with .js-show now simply appear at their natural display value when scripting is active, which is more flexible for cases where flex, inline-block, or other values are needed.

The StylesheetBytes assertion in the performance test for anonymous page loads was reduced from 1500 to 1000 to reflect the smaller CSS output. Catch noted that removing the .js-class selectors may also eliminate a style recalculation previously triggered when JavaScript added the .js class after page load.

A follow-up issue (#3568172) plans to move the .js-show/.js-hide rules into a separate library loaded only when JavaScript is active.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 21, 2025
  • First commit: April 4, 2025 (13 days later)
  • Last commit: April 22, 2026 (1 year and 4 commits later)

Key contributors

  • catch (Third and Grove)
  • nod_ (Très Bien Tech)
  • lendude (iO)
  • godotislate
  • bernardm28 (Vaultes )
  • markconroy (The Confident)
  • jwilson3 (Bluespark)

Collaboration

The path to the final solution involved a revert and a prolonged specificity debate. lendude found (comment #16069994) that after the first commit was merged, buttons in the Views wizard were showing when they should be hidden. nod_ reverted the commit and catch diagnosed the cause: Claro's .button { display: inline-block } rule was overriding the bare .js-hide selector. Three specificity approaches circulated: .js-hide.js-hide (existing Drupal core convention), .js-hide[class] (newer Drupal CMS convention), and !important. jwilson3 made the key observation (comment #16421328) that since the change also removed all display: block instances from .js-show, the utility classes now exclusively control hiding, making display: none !important semantically sound rather than a hack. Catch adopted that reasoning (comment #16421340) and switched to !important. godotislate then manually verified both the Views wizard fix and the absence of a module-filter layout-shift regression before committing.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3549730: Enable LongLineDeclaration
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 73 files, +667/−156 · php +666/−134 · dist +1/−22
  • Diff: d205a05
  • Discussion: #3549730 · 7 contributors · 29 comments
  • Followers: 8

Summary

Drupal's coding standards include a rule that flags arrays with long declarations, but this rule was not enforced across the entire codebase. This change fixes all remaining violations of the LongLineDeclaration standard by splitting long array declarations and function calls across multiple lines for better readability. The code now consistently follows the rule that lines should not exceed a certain length, making the codebase easier to read and maintain. All changes preserve existing functionality while improving code formatting.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The Drupal.Array.Array.LongLineDeclaration PHPCS sniff was previously enabled only for specific directories through include patterns in phpcs.xml.dist. This issue removed those directory restrictions and enabled the sniff globally across the entire codebase.

The changes address violations by splitting long lines in several ways: breaking array declarations across multiple lines with each element on its own line, splitting function calls with multiple parameters, and wrapping chained method calls. In batch.inc, an unnecessary array_merge() call was removed and the parameters were passed directly instead. One line in responsive_image was marked with a PHPCS ignore comment because reformatting would harm readability.

The formatting changes follow existing patterns in the codebase, prioritizing readability and consistency. No API behavior changes occurred. The diff touches approximately 170 files across core, affecting function calls with long parameter lists, array declarations, and method chaining patterns.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 1, 2025
  • First commit: March 10, 2026 (5 months later)
  • Last commit: April 22, 2026 (1 month and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • longwave (Full Fat Things)
  • dcam

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3508641: Define form elements from SDC
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Version: main
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +356/−3 · php +326/−3 · twig +18 · yml +12
  • Diff: 671d81e
  • Discussion: #3508641 · 7 contributors · 50 comments
  • Followers: 42

Summary

SDC components (#type => 'component') can now participate in form processing as input elements. Before this change, the component render element was not recognized by the Form API as an input element, so form properties like #default_value, #value, #required, and #element_validate had no effect and submitted values were not tracked. Site builders and developers can now use an SDC component directly in a buildForm() return array, and the Form API will collect its submitted value, respect #default_value and #value, enforce #required, and run #element_validate callbacks. The component's Twig template is responsible for rendering the actual HTML control using a form_state prop; the PHP layer handles all business logic. Support for #ajax and #htmx is out of scope and deferred to follow-up issues.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: SDC components (#type => 'component') now implement FormElementInterface and are recognized as Form API input elements, supporting #default_value, #value, #required, and #element_validate.

Technical details

The ComponentElement class in core/lib/Drupal/Core/Render/Element/ComponentElement.php previously carried the #[RenderElement('component')] attribute and extended RenderElementBase. The Form API's ElementInfoManager only sets #input = TRUE on elements registered with #[FormElement], so ComponentElement was invisible to FormBuilder::handleInputElement() and submitted values were never collected.

The fix changes the attribute to #[FormElement('component')] and adds implements FormElementInterface to the class declaration. ComponentElement still extends RenderElementBase rather than FormElementBase, deliberately avoiding the extra static helper methods in FormElementBase that are not applicable to components (as noted in the new docblock).

A valueCallback() method is added that returns NULL. This tells the Form API to fall back to #default_value or #value for determining the element's stored value, with no server-side transformation. Value rendering is delegated entirely to the Twig template.

In preRenderComponent(), a new block checks for a non-empty #name (which the Form API sets automatically on #input elements). When present, it populates $props['form_state']['value']['name'] and $props['form_state']['value']['required'], making those values available inside the component's Twig template via form_state.value.name and form_state.value.required. The template (not the PHP class) is responsible for rendering the <input> element, its name attribute, value, and the HTML required attribute.

A test fixture component (sdc_theme_test:input) is added under the SDC test theme, consisting of a YAML metadata file declaring an id prop and a label slot, plus a Twig template that uses form_state.value.name, form_state.value.value, and form_state.value.required to render a labeled text input. The kernel test class ComponentAsFormElementTest (in core/tests/Drupal/KernelTests/Components/) exercises rendering and submission with #default_value, #value, #required, #slots, #props (including attributes), and #element_validate callbacks.

The design principle articulated in the thread (comment 16133116 by pdureau) is that SDC components own the UI logic; callbacks like #element_validate are added at the PHP/applicative layer by code that returns a #type => 'component' element, not declared inside the component YAML itself. #ajax/#htmx support is explicitly out of scope for this change.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 24, 2025
  • Committed: April 22, 2026 (1 year later)

Key contributors

  • pdureau
  • grimreaper (Smile)
  • d34dman (Factorial GmbH)
  • nod_ (Très Bien Tech)
  • mglaman (Acquia)
  • catch (Third and Grove)

Collaboration

The issue originated at DDD 2025 (Drupal Developer Days), where grimreaper worked on a proof of concept and shared a screen recording demo of a working SDC-based form element at the end of the event (comment #16074581). d34dman joined the effort and made their first commit to the fork, contributing the initial test coverage. A key design clarification came from a discussion between pdureau and d34dman (comment #16133116): #element_validate and similar processing callbacks belong at the PHP/applicative layer, not inside the SDC component itself, which resolved an open question about where business logic should live. mglaman reviewed the MR and confirmed all concerns had been addressed (comment #16557654). pdureau committed the final result.

item.title: #3015812: Introduce new Theme extension object and properly deprecate REGIONS_VISIBLE and REGIONS_ALL
item.description:
• Project: Drupal Core
  • Type: Major task
  • Version: 11.x-dev
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 26 files, +360/−89 · php +360/−89
  • Diff: 0f4d171
  • Discussion: #3015812 · 29 contributors · 181 comments
  • Followers: 39

Summary

Drupal 11.4 introduces a dedicated Theme extension object and properly deprecates the long-standing system_region_list() and system_default_region() procedural functions along with the REGIONS_ALL and REGIONS_VISIBLE constants. Sites that call those functions or use those constants in custom or contrib code should migrate to the new OOP API on the Theme object. No user-facing behavior changes.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • API change: ThemeHandlerInterface::addTheme() now requires a \Drupal\Core\Extension\Theme argument instead of the base Extension; callers must pass a Theme object.
  • Deprecation: system_region_list() (removed drupal:13.0.0), REGIONS_ALL/REGIONS_VISIBLE global constants and BlockRepositoryInterface equivalents (removed drupal:13.0.0), BlockListBuilder::systemRegionList() and BlockDeleteForm::systemRegionList() (removed drupal:12.0.0), BlockController::getVisibleRegionNames() (removed drupal:12.0.0). Use ThemeHandlerInterface::getTheme()->listAllRegions(), ->listVisibleRegions(), or ->getDefaultRegion() instead.
  • New API: New \Drupal\Core\Extension\Theme class (extends Extension, marked @final) with listAllRegions(), listVisibleRegions(), and getDefaultRegion() methods; new protected subClassExtension() hook in ExtensionList for returning typed extension subclasses.

Technical details

The root problem was twofold. First, #2807785 deprecated REGIONS_VISIBLE and REGIONS_ALL in the System module but pointed callers at \Drupal\block\BlockRepositoryInterface constants, which was conceptually wrong: regions belong to themes, not blocks. Second, no typed Theme-specific object existed, so region logic lived in procedural helpers (system_region_list(), system_default_region()) rather than on the extension object itself.

New Theme class (core/lib/Drupal/Core/Extension/Theme.php): Extends Extension and is marked @final (extending is unsupported). Its constructor accepts $root, $pathname, array $info, and an optional $filename. It exposes three new public methods: listAllRegions() returns all regions as TranslatableMarkup[] keyed by machine name; listVisibleRegions() filters out entries listed in info['regions_hidden']; getDefaultRegion() returns the machine name of the first visible region.

ExtensionList::subClassExtension() hook (core/lib/Drupal/Core/Extension/ExtensionList.php): A new protected subClassExtension(Extension $extension): Extension method is added to the base list class and called inside doList() after createExtensionInfo(). The loop is updated to key-track extensions so the replacement object is stored back. The base implementation is a no-op pass-through; subclasses override it to return a typed subclass.

ThemeExtensionList::subClassExtension() override: Returns new Theme($this->root, $extension->getPathname(), $extension->info, $extension->getExtensionFilename()), converting the raw Extension into a Theme for every entry in the theme list.

ThemeHandlerInterface and ThemeHandler: addTheme() now declares Theme $theme (hard break; the old Extension type is no longer accepted). listInfo(), rebuildThemeData(), and getTheme() return/yield Theme objects. A guard in ThemeHandler::listInfo() adds instanceof Theme to the existing isset($list[$theme]) check so stale cache entries containing bare Extension objects do not cause errors.

Deprecations (all drupal:11.4.0, removed drupal:13.0.0 unless noted):

  • system_region_list() — removed drupal:13.0.0. Triggers E_USER_DEPRECATED and delegates to Theme::listAllRegions() or Theme::listVisibleRegions().
  • system_default_region() — same timeline (mentioned in issue body; not visible in this partial diff).
  • REGIONS_ALL and REGIONS_VISIBLE global constants.
  • BlockRepositoryInterface::REGIONS_ALL and BlockRepositoryInterface::REGIONS_VISIBLE — removed drupal:13.0.0.
  • BlockListBuilder::systemRegionList() — removed drupal:12.0.0. ThemeHandlerInterface injected as optional fifth constructor argument with deprecation shim.
  • BlockDeleteForm::systemRegionList() — same timeline. ThemeHandlerInterface injected via new explicit constructor and ContainerInterface::create().
  • BlockController::getVisibleRegionNames() — removed drupal:12.0.0.

Callers updated inside core: BlockHooks::rebuild(), BlockHooks::themeInitialize(), block_theme_initialize(), Block::preSave(), Block::validateRegion(), and BlockListBuilder::buildBlocksForm() all now resolve regions via $themeHandler->getTheme($name)->listAllRegions() or ->listVisibleRegions() / ->getDefaultRegion().

Tests: Kernel tests that previously instantiated BlockHooks directly (new BlockHooks()) are updated to resolve it from the container (\Drupal::service(BlockHooks::class)) because it now requires ThemeHandlerInterface via constructor injection. Package Manager tests that called $theme_handler->addTheme($extension) now wrap the Extension in a new Theme(...) first.

Upgrade

Replace calls to the deprecated procedural functions:

// Before
$all     = system_region_list($theme_name);
$visible = system_region_list($theme_name, REGIONS_VISIBLE);
$default = system_default_region($theme_name);

// After
$theme_obj = \Drupal::service('theme_handler')->getTheme($theme_name);
$all       = $theme_obj->listAllRegions();
$visible   = $theme_obj->listVisibleRegions();
$default   = $theme_obj->getDefaultRegion();

Replace references to the deprecated constants:

// Before
if ($show === REGIONS_VISIBLE) { ... }
if ($show === \Drupal\block\BlockRepositoryInterface::REGIONS_VISIBLE) { ... }

// After – pass the Theme object directly and call the appropriate method
$visible = $theme_obj->listVisibleRegions();
$all     = $theme_obj->listAllRegions();

If your class extends BlockListBuilder and overrides systemRegionList(), migrate to injecting ThemeHandlerInterface and calling getTheme()->listAllRegions() or ->listVisibleRegions() directly.

If you call ThemeHandlerInterface::addTheme(), you must now pass a \Drupal\Core\Extension\Theme object:

// Before
$theme_handler->addTheme($extension);

// After
use Drupal\Core\Extension\Theme;
$theme = new Theme($app_root, $extension->getPathname(), $info_array, $extension->getExtensionFilename());
$theme_handler->addTheme($theme);

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 23, 2018
  • Committed: April 20, 2026 (7 years and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • alexpott (Acro Commerce)
  • andypost (Skilld)
  • vacho (Skilld)
  • voleger (Drupal Ukraine Community)
  • hardik_patel_12 (QED42)
  • nikitagupta (Material)
  • jofitz
  • smustgrave (Mobomo)
  • quietone (PreviousNext)
  • viappidu
  • ameymudras (Salsa Digital)
  • ravi.shankar (OpenSense Labs)
  • suresh prabhu parkala (Specbee)
  • akram khan (Material)
  • xjm (xjm)

Collaboration

alexpott opened the issue and drove almost all iterations, cycling through three distinct approaches before landing on the Theme subclass strategy: first putting methods on ThemeHandler, then a ThemeInfo value object, then the current Theme extends Extension approach. tim.plunkett pushed back on expanding ThemeHandlerInterface directly, which contributed to the value-object direction. phenaproxima and markhalliwell (markcarver) both independently recommended marking Theme as @final or @internal to avoid locking in an incomplete API, and the final patch honours that with @final and the "no BC for subclasses" note. The issue sat stalled across multiple Drupal minor-version cycles (8.7 through 11) due to repeated re-roll requirements; voleger performed several rerolls that kept it alive and ultimately got it to a committable state.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.
